questions.question: Which of these behaviors is typical of an Influential (I) personality?

options.text: Asking detailed questions about data security
Talking about their weekend, using emojis, and going on tangents
Sitting quietly and taking notes
Demanding a 10% discount immediately

questions.explanation: I-types are social, expressive, and relationship-focused. They communicate with high energy and often blur the line between business and personal topics.

questions.question: What is the biggest risk when selling to an I-Type?

options.text: They will argue with you about the price
They will demand too many technical features
They will express excitement ('False Positive') but then ghost you
They will refuse to talk to you

questions.explanation: I-types are people-pleasers and enthusiasts. They hate saying 'no' to your face, so they say 'This is great!' even if they don't intend to buy. This is the 'False Positive' trap.

questions.question: How should you tailor your demo for an I-Type?

options.text: Focus on specific features, settings, and configurations
Show the back-end architecture and API docs
Focus on the 'Big Picture,' Vision, and Ease of Use
Skip the demo and just send a contract

questions.explanation: I-types buy the *vision* of a better future. They want to see the result and how 'cool' or 'easy' it is. Details bore them.

questions.question: What is the best way to prevent an I-Type from ghosting you?

options.text: Send them 5 follow-up emails
Anchor the next step to another Person (e.g., 'Let's show your team')
Threaten to expire the discount
Ask them to sign an NDA

questions.explanation: I-types care about people. If the next step involves a colleague or team member, they feel a social obligation to show up. If it's just 'thinking about it,' they will forget.

questions.question: Which emotional trigger works best for an I-Type?

options.text: Fear of missing out (FOMO) and Social Status
Risk mitigation and Safety
Accuracy and Logic
Control and Power

questions.explanation: I-types want to be seen as leaders, innovators, and 'in the know.' Telling them 'Competitor X is doing this' or 'You will look like a hero' triggers their desire for status.
